label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to solve this problem. So, Jane was born on the last day of February in 2001. That would be February 28, 2001, right? Because 2001 is not a leap year. Wait, leap years are every 4 years, so 2000 was a leap year, then 2004, 2008, etc. So 2001's February had 28 days. Today is her 16-year-old birthday. So Jane's birthday is on February 28 every year. So if today is her 16th birthday, that means today's date is February 28, 2001 + 16 years, which would be February 28, 2017. Wait, because adding 16 years to 2001 would be 2017. So today is February 28, 2017. Now the question is asking for the date one year ago from today. So subtract one year from today's date (Feb 28, 2017). That would be February 28, 2016. But wait, 2016 is a leap year. But since we're subtracting a year, not adding, does that affect anything? Let me think. If today is February 28, 2017, then one year ago would be February 28, 2016. Even though 2016 had a February 29, since the original date is February 28, we don't need to worry about leap years here. Because if you subtract a year from Feb 28, 2017, you just get Feb 28, 2016, regardless of whether 2016 is a leap year or not. Because the date isn't affected by the existence of February 29. So the answer would be 02/28/2016. Looking at the options, option C is 02/28/2016. So the answer should be C. Let me double-check. Jane's birthday is on Feb 28 every year. If today is her 16th birthday, then she was born on Feb 28, 2001. So today is Feb 28, 2017. One year ago from today would be Feb 28, 2016. So the correct date is 02/28/2016, which is option C. Yep, that makes sense. The other options don't seem right. For example, option A is 02/28/2015, which is two years before today.
